titleStmt.title: Letter from John Dillon to Lady Elizabeth Mathew, 25 April - 1 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: John Dillon

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This letter is from Irish Parliamentary Party leader and MP John Dillon (1851-1927) to his mother in law Lady Elizabeth Mathew (d. 1933). This is a 110 page letter written between 25 April (the second day of the Rising) and 1 May 1916 recounting events in during the Rising. The letter contains fascinating detail about the events in Dublin including those experienced by Dillon himself as well as news, reports, rumours and gossip given to Dillon by family and colleagues. Dillon was the only Irish Parliamentary Party leader in Dublin during the Rising and spent the week confined to his house in North Great George's Street, a short distance from the rebel headquarters in the GPO. He went on to denounced the execution of the leaders in Westminster in a now famous speech on 11 May 1916.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
2 North Georges Street.
Dublin Monday 25 April 1916
4.30 My dear Lady Mathew
We have had a
wild day here — But the
family are all safe —
and for the time being
out of the fighting zone.
At 12.30 Myles rushed
in very hot & excited
and told me that
the Sinn Féiners — had
entrenched themselves in
Stephens Green — and
were marching on the Castle.
He had followed them
for some distance —
and then when firing commenced
very wisely ran away —
He also told us that
they were in possession
of the General Post Office and that fighting was
going on in O'Connell
St. Standing at the
door here —we could hear
heavy firing going on
in various parts of the
City. Happily Nano
was at home — But
Jamie and Brian had
just gone out & proceeded
down O'Connell St. —
and Shaun was at
12o'c Mass at Adam & Eve —
on the Quays — Jim
had gone out to the
Park with a school
friend — You may
imagine I was rather
unhappy till they all
returned safely — which they all
did in about half an
hour. Jamie & Brian were
caught just below the Parnell monument — and when
the firing began — they took
refuge in the Hammam
Hotel — and then took
advantage of a lull to
escape home. Shaun had various
adventures — but
finally got home
untouched — He had
walked up O'Connell St. —
to the corner of Abbey St —
before he realised that
there was any trouble
in the Post Office —
Theobald is spending
the day at Glenageary —
and I have sent him
a telephone message to
stay with his friends
for the night. So far
this street has been
perfectly quiet —
But the wildest rumours
reach us from time to
time — from passers by —
we are told that
the Castle has been taken
also the Bank of Ireland. Tuesday 26 April Here I was interrupted
yesterday — I find there is no
possibility of getting a letter —
or telegram through —
but I shall continue this
narrative as it may interest
you — when this miserable
business is all over — At 4oc Mary Dillon
came in — she had made
her way down as far
as Nelson's Pillar — Shortly
before she got there a
squadron of the 6th Lancers
had gone down Rutland
Sq — towards O'Connell —
for which purpose Heaven —
9820 3
knows —Mary saw two
dead horses lying in
front of the General Post
Office — and was
informed by a man in
the crowd — that the
Commanding Officer of the
Lancers had been shot
from the Post Office &
carried off in a dying
condition —The man
then added that deleted textthe
Lancers turned tail. O'Connell Street was
crowded by a perfectly
orderly crowd apparently
taking a big interest
in the proceedings. The inhabitants of the
Slums were busy looting
the shops. All deleted textyesterday afternoon
and again this morning
a steady stream of
women — girls & young
children have passed
up the street laden with loot — clothes —
boots, boxes of sweets
&c. It's It is horrible
to see some well dressed
respectable looking
girls — laden with loot —
Just now a woman passed
with a baby's perambulator
piled with boots. Ismay
informed us that before
she left the Hospital a
child was brought in
with a bullet through
its arm & the little
creature had collected
several boxes of sweets
at Noblets shop
9820 4
and was making off
with them when it was
shot. The man who
brought in the child
was careful to say that
it was shot by a
Sinn Féiner and not
by the soldiers. Desultory firing all round
this house went on
all the afternoon and
evening — and it was
impossible to get any definite
news of what is taking place. At 5 o'c a lady — who
had an appointment with
Mr & Mrs O'Farrell
called — She informed me
that she had motored
in from the country —
was stopped at a
barricade outside Dublin —
by a youth with a
revolver — Her motor
was commandeered —
and when she asked
what had happened —
The youth replied — he"we asked for a Republic.
They would not give it
so we took it"! I should have said
that Mary reported that
large placards were
posted in O'Connell St.
announcing the foundation
of the Irish Republic. —
And the new Irish
Republican Tricolour was
flying over the G.P.O.
Harold Cochran - Mrs
Tuthill's grandson — came
in and ifinformed that
they had a telephone message from his aunt —
9820 5
from Vincent's Hospital to say
that 20 dead had been
carried into that hospital
already. At seven oclock a
furious fusillade of rifle
fire & machine guns
broke out and lasted
for almost twenty minutes
— we could get no information
where it was or what was the
result. I was very much afraid
that the slum people
would break into the
public houses and that
the city at night would
be in the hands of howling
drunken mob. All So I kept the children
all sitting up till
12.30 —Then the
city was absolutely quiet —
we all went to bed —
About 1.30 a furious
fusillade suddenly
broke out again —
machine guns & rifles —
and some of the firing
seemed to be quite
close in Gardiners Row —
or Rutland Sq. —
We were all out
of bed again &
assembled in Nano's
room & you would
really imagine that you
were in the middle of a battle. The appearance of the
street as viewed from
this house has been
most extraordinary —
a constant flow of
people - largely women & young girls —
9820 6
going & coming without
the slightest appearance
of excitement — more
than double as many
people as one would
see in these streets on any
ordinary day — The only
thing remarkable being their
absolute silence — No
one seems to speak.
In the intervals between
the outbursts of firing
there is almost eerie
silence — you could hear
a pin drop — Then
a few scattered shots —
Then a fierce fusillade —
Then again silence —
And occasional shrieks
& shots in the distance.
At 2oc we all went
to bed again & slept
soundly till 7oc — when
we were roused by
a terrific fusillade —
This time there was the
sound of heavy guns —
and something that
sounded like a big
explosion. On looking out while
dressing I was amazed
to see the bread cart
— milk cart going
round as usual —
Our milk man came at
9.15 —and brought
a budget of news —
he was driving along
behind two bread
vans & the Volunteers
held up the bread vans
and while the men
with the bread
vans he turned tail
& made off pursued
9820 7
by the Volunteers.
We laid in an extra
supply. He told us
that a racing man had come
up of from Cork yesterday
in a motor all the
way - that the insurrection
has broken out in Cork
and in Kerry — and
that on his way he
was held up at
several barricades —
He also told us that
all thro the night
the Sinn Féiners had been
firing on the Constabulary
Barracks in Phoenix
Park — and that
they had taken the
barracks this morning.
After breakfast I took
stock of provisions in the
house & sent out Lizzie
to buy all she could get
& and I am glad to say
she has just arrived
accompanied by two
boys with large stores — 3oc Theo arrived safely at
1oc — He had a
rather adventurous journey —
had to go round by Bray and
Harcourt Street — as
Westland Row in the hands
of the enemy. When he reached
DoCollege Green — he found
the Military in possession
of T.C.D. — firing out
of the windows up Dame
Street — and had to wait
for a lull in the firing
before he crossed — StAt
Nelsons Pillar two barbed
wires were stretched across the
Street — and he had to
stoop under the wires to
pass.
9820 8
O'Connell Street was
still crowded with
people. At 2.30 Christie
Redmond M - Devlin's Sec —
called — He had collected
a great mass of information —
Had witnessed some severe
fighting — in which the soldiers
suffered severely. Among the items of information
he brought me was that
two priests had been shot.
That it was rumoured that
at Beggars Bush Barracks
the Sinn Féiners had occupied
a row of houses opposite
the Barracks and were
firing into it — And
that inside the Barracks
the two Irish Regiments
occupying the Barracks
the Royal Irish and
the Connaught Rangers —
had fallen out and were
firing on each other.
He told me that the Castle
had notbeen taken —
but was still occupied
by the Military — and
that he had seen the
Militarysoldiers on top
of the City Hall — from
which they had driven the
Sinn Féiners.
That the S.F. s had
blown up a bridge on
the Great Northern line
at Donabate —and had
there met the troops coming
from Belfast by rail —
and driven them back
and taken a machine
gun from them. We are now informed on
fairly good authority that
there are two Warships
in the Bay — and that
the Authorities have
concentrated 9000 troops —
and are going to clear
the streets with Artillery
commencing at 4oc this afternoon — Many wild rumours are
flying about — That
500 men from Kerry arrived
this morning to reinforce
the Volunteers — that
6000 Kerry men & 500 from
Waterford are marching from
on Dublin. That the
Germans landed in Tralee
yesterday — that the German
submarines are in Belfast
Lough — and landed in Belfast
yesterday — and attacked
the Belfast Post Office. Wednesday 10.30 Wasn't up till 12oc —
last night — then as the
city was fairly quiet we
all went to bed — looters
continued to stream up
Georges Street — far into the night
laden with bacon deleted text-
— fruit etc. — and
could be heard discussing
their booty with great
satisfaction — a considerable
number of drunken women
and girls were heard in
the street. I slept soundly & was
woken shortly after seven
by firing — at about
a quarter to 8oc — a violent
fusillade broke out —
machine guns, rifle &
field guns — It appeared
to be very near — lasted
for about half an
hour — then died away.
When I came down Mrs.
Dolan told me that a
woman had just told
her that about Jacobs
factory the City was in
9820 10
ruins. Standing at the door
a man passing on the
opposite side of the
street saluted me &
asked me — How are
you Sir" "Very well I replied —
as well as one can be
in such times —
To which he replied —
If you keep your head
it will be all right.
Large numbers of people
walking about — without
slightest appearance of
fear or panic — A
good many apparently
going down towards Marlboro
Street and O'Connell St.
to see what is going
on. Desultory firing has
been going on ever since
8oc. Jim was told
by Blake next door
neighbour that his
brother had been down
to the Quays — that
Liberty Hall was laid flat — and
that there was a gunboat in the
Liffey — which had bombarded
the Hall. What an extraordinary
experience it is — to be
isolated from the whole
world — not to be able
to get the slightest atom
of reliable information
as to what is going on.
Not to know what
has happened in streets
within a few hundred
yards — and to listen
for two days to the
firing close at hand
without being able
9820 11
to ascertain where it
has taken place or what
has been the result. Found last night that
Lizzie really obtained
very little supplies —
We have sent her &
Dolan out again in
search of supplies.
Last night the gas was cut
off — and we were reduced
to lamps & candles. Friday 28th Wrote nothing yesterday —
It was one of the worst
days we had —
About 6.30 4.30 there was
a tremendous fusillade —
accompanied by a good deal
of firing from field
guns. Large fires in
O'Connell Street during
the night - Lawrences
has been burned — during
the day the fires spread
and the D.B.C —
fell in. Fortunately the
wind was not blowing
in this direction. Did not allow any of
children out to Mass
as there was continuous
desultory firing all round.
In the course of the day
we were told that
deleted textthe authorities
had given the Insurgents
in the G.P.O. up to
3.30 to surrender - after
that they would proceed
to shell the G.P.O. In the course of the
forenoon — there were
a succession of furious
fusillades in Great Britain
Street direction — and
from the steps I 9820 12
could see deleted texta picket
of Soldiers with fixed
bayonets moving about
in Great Britain Street
at foot of this street.
I sent Theo over to Belvedere
to find out whether
Jesuits had any news —
Father Fahey S.J.
called about 12<hi rend="superscript'oc.
He had been a good deal
round the city — and
was the first man
from whom I got
definite news. He is
in charge of Dressing
Station in Dorset Street —
which was established in
Chemist shop Cahills at
corner of Dorset St — and Nth Circular.
The proprietor of which
is out with the Volunteers.
I was very much
alarmed — thinking the
military had selected the
house on purpose —
Father Fahey reassured
me — and said the
selection of house was
a mere fluke—
A machine gun was
placed on roof — and
then the officer in
charge proposed to
hang out the Red Cross
flag — Father Fahey
remonstrated — and the
officer said — Oh — I
forgot all about the
gun. Afterwards they
moved the dressing station
to another house. Father CahillFahey informed
me that up to the present
the casualties were not all that heavy —
9820 13
and bore no appreciable
proportion to the prodigious
expenditure of ammunition.
Two cases had been treated
in the Dorset Street dressing
station. George Fothell
told me on Wednesday that
up to then only two dead
had been brought into
Vincents. Father Fahey said that
several dead bodies were
lying in Stephens Green —
He confirmed the story of
priests goinghaving gone
into the G.P.O. — heard
confessions of all
the Garrison — found them
full of enthusiasm and
confidence — quite convinced
that they were in the right
fighting for Ireland —
and ready to die for
the cause. One man
dying man was
anointed - and said to
the priest who anointed
him that he was proud
to die for Ireland.
Father F. told me that
when he first met the
Officers & soldiers
coming into Dublin —
they were in a very great
state of extreme
nervousness — under the
impression that they
were coming amongst
a solidly hostile population
— that they would be
sniped from every house —
and would be attacked
by hostile crowds on all
sides. He Father F. had
assured them that they
were under a wholly false
impression — that they
would find the great
majority of the population
friendly — and he said
that the officers had
now come to realise that
in this respect he was quite right. At 4.30 the big guns
began —one from Parnell
Monument — and others
from other side of river —
shelling the G.P.O.
This was accompanied
by firm machine gun
firing — all the
evening we could get
no definite or reliable
information as to results.
At about 7oc there was a ring
at the hall door — and
on opening it — I found
a correspondent of the Manchester Guardian —
He had left Manchester
on Tuesday evening —
and survived after many
difficulties in reaching
Dublin by way of
Belfast & Drogheda —
by road from Drogheda to Dublin —
From him I heard some
outside news — up to
Tuesday evening & including
news of naval raid on Lowestoft —
and naval engagement
resulting which appears
to have been uncertain.
First news of Dublin
outbreak reached
Manchester at midday
Tuesday — very brief account
from official Press bureau —
followed by a statement
from Mr. Birrell. Saturday 29 April
At night the fires in 8.15
O'Connell St — and
Dominic St Church
in the direction of Linen Hall
Barracks were so terrible
that I did not let the
children go to bed —
9820 15
we all sat up till
3oc — I then arranged
with the to sit
up in relays till full
daylight — At six
Kevin called me to say
that fires were dying down
and there was no danger
to house —and we
slept till 9 — breakfast
at 9.45 — About
8oc there was a raging
fusilade — the big guns
going again. George On Friday (yesterday)
foreman of Fothell called
in & told me that
the authorities had given
up to 4.30 to the
garrison in G.P.O.
to surrender — after that
they proposed to shell
the place.
At four 4.30
there was a tremendous
rattle of machine guns
and — considerable
firing of field guns —
then a lull — Later
we could see the G.
P.O. burning fiercely .
The firing round this
house was much
more constant & violent
on Friday — than on
any previous day —
A proclamation had
been issued on Thursday
— warning people not to
congregate on the streets and
to be in doors between
between6am and
7.30P.M. & 6A.M.
And on yesterday for
the first time the streets
became very deserted.
About 5oc — sitting in
the study — a quantity of
9820 16
bullets passed the windows
& struck Belvedere
College — After a bit
there was a lull and a
ring came to the door
— I opened it — and
found a small boy from
the Plaza Hotel —
who said I was called
up to the Castle —
After some hesitation
I decided to go —
I was called in at Moloney's
Hotel — next door —
men standing in the
hall begged me not
to venture — saying
that bullets were coming
up Georges St — and
also over the wall
at the back of the houses
— and along Gardiners
Row from Findlater's Church.
I tried to use the
telephone at Moloney's
— but failed to connect. —
So I ran across
the Plaza — where I
had a short talk with Nathan — He Sunday 30 April
10oc
I was interrupted yesterday —
Nathan told me he had
an urgent message from
Redmond saying he was
eagerly awaiting Miss O'Brien
with important message from
Dillon — and asking Castle
whether they could communicate
with Dillon — I said I
had sent Miss O'Brien
on Tuesday night —Nathan
replied he knew that — but
she had been quite unable
to get across town. I asked him to repeat
to R my urgent request
9820 17
that he should remain
in London until I had
an opportunity of communicating
fully with him He told me Birrell was
in the Castle —
Asked me how are you?
I replied all right —
The fires in the City are
terrible — To which he replied
I know that — I then said
they have been firing up
Georges Street allthis
afternoon, to which he replied
I am aware of that also.
No further communication passed. A man in the Hall of
the Plaza told me
that Verdun had fallen.
I had a few words with
Radcliffe of the M.G.
He seemed very much
depressed — disheartened
at his position — had not
been able to collect any
news. A young lady spoke to
me — and said that
in Barry's Hotel — they
were in a terrible position.
There were 30 people in the
Hotel and only one
loaf —and they could
not get any bread —
she had succeeded in
getting into communication
with Captain Kelly on
the telephone — and he
had promised to have
either bread or flour
delivered in this quarter
tomorrow Saturday — While
I was speaking at
telephone some bullets had
9820 18
passed up Gardiners Row.
After waiting for a bit
in the hall I made a
bolt for home. On my
way I enquired at Moloneys
Hotel as to possibility of
communicating with West of
Mayo — But was told
it was absolutely impossible
to do so. 10.20
Just at the moment the
milkman arrived — he said
he was talking to a
Major on his way in
and was told by him that
the troops were now getting
ready to sweep the
country — and that the
worst thing they had to
face was Dolphins Barn.
As I was going to the
door to open it to speak
to the milkman a posse
of about 10 policemen
came down Gardiners Row
and passed on towards
Findlaters Church — The
first sight of a policeman's
uniform I have
seen had since Monday
morning last. Very strange
the feeling of satisfaction
and joy with which I
saw the uniforms — as a
sign of returning security
and peace.—
The milkman said that
there had been a terrible
naval battle off Lowestoft
that 14 German and
8 British Ships had
been sunk. — That fifteen
Zeppelins had attacked
London — and that
London was a wreck. So far I have not got
a scrap of any news of the
outside world — except
scraps — rumours collected in this way.
982019
Shawn came in just now. —
I had sent him to Dominic
St. to ascertain whether the
Church is open & whether
it is quiet & safe in that
region — He reported the
Church open — morning
congregation — all quite
peaceable — He saw Father
Costelloe — who was eager
to learn whether I knew
what had really happened.
Shawn said I knew nothing
more than the ordinary
man in the street.
Father Costelloe said that
he was informed that the
casualties amongst the
soldiers were enormous. I take up again the
narrative at Friday evening
where I broke off to deal
with the arrival of the milkman.
On Friday night the
fires were still raging
in O'Connell Street
& in two or three other
places in the distance.
But as there was
notno apparent danger
of their spreading to
this Street — we
went to bed early .
during the night
At night - bef
we sat in the parlour
as the firing continued in
Georges St -—except
from desultory firing the
street was as silent
as a Churchyard.
During the night I
slept soundly — But
in the morning I was
informed by Nano — that
there had been a fierce
battle in the street.
9820 20
It seems the soldiers had
during the night opened
fire on some of the houses
in Georges Street in which
snipers were lodged.
Fortunately no bullets
struck this house. Saturday morning was
as usual — with a good
deal of firing .
About 12oc Father Fahy
called, and said he had come
to consult me — as to what
could be done to get bread
for the people . The women
were rushing to him from all
sides calling for bread
and saying that their
children were dying of
hunger. He told me
a long tale of his adventure
with the Military Officer
— It seems they will not
allow anyone to pass the
cordons — except under
very strict conditions —
and it has not been
possible for ana great
many to get to
Kennedys shop . I advised
him on a course of
proceeding — During his
visit he told me a
very pathetic story — A
young man had stopped him shortlduring his morning
his morning round & saying I
want to shake hands with
you — aren't we doing
splendidly? — he
then explained that he had been
at home to spend the night
with his family and was now
on his way to relieve
Guard — Father Fahy
remonstrated with him using
very strong — and in rather
cruel language — said
he regarded their conduct
9820 21
as murder — asked him
was he married — To this
the man replied that he had
a wife & three young children.
And you are leaving them
to fight for a lost cause
— said Father Fahy —
a cause in which you can
do no good — but a vast
deal of mischief —
At this thetears streamed
down the face of the
unhappy youth —
But he was quite fine
—said he must do his duty —
that spiritually he was
all right and ready to
die — as he had been
to the sacraments and
was doing no wrong —
and what object can
you hope to achieve
said Father F. — "Some
of us must die — But
we shall save Ireland
from Conscription.
Father F. then pressed him
to come into Berkeley
St. Church and say
three Hail Maries — But
he refused to and
went on his way. All seemed going
usual till lunch dinner time.
1.30 — After that
there was a complete
cessation of firing
and something had
happened - people began
to emerge from the houses —
and gradually the streets
became almost crowded
— groups eagerly talking —
women moving about
looking for bread &
provisions of all kinds.
At various times through
the forenoon milk
carts had appeared in the
Street —the arrival of each
9820 22
cart being the occasion
of an extraordinary scene —
women & girls & servant-maid
rushing at the cart with
bowls — jugs etc. — and
scrambling for milk. About 3oc standing on
the steps — Jemma who
had been out for milk
called — look — look
the soldiers are advancing
up the Street with bayonets
fixed — I looked and
saw an extraordinary sight
three soldiers — two
of them bodiminutive boys —
with arms of any kind —
and the third carrying a
rifle and bayonet in an
awkward fashion as
if half afraid of it
walked up past our
door — and turned into
Gardiners Place — some
one than told us that
a woman asked one of
the soldiers as they
passed — Is it peace?
and he replied
Yes peace has been
proclaimed. For a
considerable time we
got no more news —
but the streets became
more & more crowded
And there was a
perfect cessation of firing
at about 5oc. Hannon
& McMahon came in —
They had little news —
Hannon told me of
his wholly unsuccessful
attempts to reach me
during the last three
days — There had been
a great deal of fighting
out his way — in the
9820 23
direction of Fairview.
He had had a talk over
the telephone with
Lord Basil Blackwood —
who told him that
there was trouble in
several parts of the
Country — mentioned
Louth, Meath (!!!) —
But said the most
serious case was in
Galway.
Miss O'Brien called up at
6oc. She informed me
that she had several
attempts to get across
town — but had failed —
had each time to come
back — she had been
fired at twice. She
had determined however
to deleted texttry again that
night — and was
confident of success —
I went over again the
message I wanted her to give
R. Miss O'B. told me
that fight in Dublin
was abandoned —the
Volunteers having
surrendered unconditionally.
This she had been told
by officers in the
Gresham — There are
several officers staying
at the Gresham —
No more definite news
last night — but the
usual crop of rumours.
I sent out the boys
to collect news from
groups in the Street
They brought in the
most contradictory .
Some said that the
fight was not at all
over — that it was
only a 24 hour truce
9820 24
to bury the dead.
Father Costelloe the
Dominican told Theo
that there was no truth
in statement of unconditional surrender —
In the country the
Volunteers had no idea
of surrendering . But
were determined to fight to
the last. In one group
was informed by a man
that Mr John Dillon
had gone down and
got between the opposing
forces and made
peace. — Another
man standing by
said there was not a
word of truth in the
statement —and that
if M.Dillon had
gone down his brains
would have been blown out.
At 9oc Mr.Radcliffe
of the M.G. called
in. He was in a
very nervous and depressed
condition and very
anxious to get out of
Dublin except to
confirm statement that
the Volunteers at G.
P.O. had surrendered
unconditionally. We had a
general talk over the
situation, and I asked
him to let Scott know
my views as to future
policy — And as to
genesis of present situation.
So far as it is possible
to form a view at
present stage. A ferocious fire
was raging between
the Rotunda & Arnotts —
9820 25
and it seemed that
the whole of the block
between Great Britain
Street —Henry Street
must have been burned out.
But for this the night
passed quietly and we
all passedwent to bed
early & slept soundly.
I got up at 5oc and
looked out. A most
lovely Spring morning —
Everything quiet and
peaceful — as if nothing
had happened out of the
normal. At 11.10 — we started
for 11.30 Mass at
Dominick — I never
saw a more delightful
Spring day —the Green
leaves just bursting out
in the Square - the old
pear tree on the C.D.
Office looking more
beautiful than it ever
looked before. The
Street, crowded with
people — for the most
part looking quite
cheerful & unconcerned —
screams of children —
rushing about in high
spirits at recovered
liberty — coming out
of the Church after Mass
Father Masterson spoke to me
and I leaned against the
Church & spoke to him
for some time. He
told me had himself
witnessed the Volunteers
surrendering on Saturday
evening. They were
marched up to the Parnell
Monument with a white
flag flying — At the
Monument they laid
down their arms &
9820 26
accoutrements and
were then marched off
as prisoners — in custody
of soldiers & police.
He said the fight was
notall over as —
The Four Courts, the
College of Surgeons — and
other places were holding
out — But he thought
that practically the fight
in Dublin was at an end
At 12.20 Father Masterson
called — and we had
a further talk — He
was anxious as to executions
etc. Theo brought in a rumour
during the morning that
60 prisoners had been shot
in the Castle early this
morning ; later on this
rumour was disposed of
by an interview between
Eily Cochran and the
officer in charge of the
Barracks at the bottom of
this Street — He told
her that they had
taken 400 Sinn Féin
prisoners last night
and that he would
have shot them all
immediately — but this
would not be allowed
by the Government —
two of his fellow
officers had been killed.
About 12.45 — came
a soldier from the Castle
with a letter for me Monday 1 May 1916
10oc
The night has been absolutely
quiet — we went to bed
at 11oc — and I slept soundly
till six o clock. I resume the narration of
yesterday —
Nathan's letter covered
a letter from R. asking for
9820 27
information. I wrote
at some length in reply —
asked the soldier whether the
City was quiet between here
and the Castle - He replied
perfectly quiet 'Sir'. At about 4oc Hannon
& McMahon came —
they had no fresh news —
Hannon has now got a
general pass to enter & leave
Dublin. I arranged with
him to tell Flynn to call to
see me — also to see
Muldoon with message
from me — Muldoon
is at Sutton. Hannon
is to call again today .
I think I shall not let
him go to London till
Tuesday. As Miss O'Brien
did not communicate with
me yesterday, I conclude
she got away on Saturday
night.
While we were at tea —
6.30 — George Fottrell
came in & told us that
the College of Surgeons
had surrendered during the
day. Also that he had
seen a copy of the Sunday
pictorial — That Verdun
had not fallen — and
there was no reference
in that paper to the
Zeppelin raid — nor
to the battle in North Sea.
10.30 The milkman arrived —
He reported the military
cordons more strict than
ever. Large bodies of
soldiers went out last
night in direction of Swords
and then accompanied
by Guinness Lorry Wagons
who had been commandeered. 9820 28 There was a short
burst of firing in that direction
during the night —
He also said there was
heavy firing out at sea
off Clontarf. James informed me this
morning that he had
overheard men in several
groups statin yesterday
stating confidently that it
was Mr John Dillon who
made the peace — God
bless him — sure he is a
great man — he went
down to the G.P.O.
and arranged for peace.
